g.title: Attach Motion Sensor to Base Plate
g.desc: This instruction shows attaching the "Motion Sensor" to the round plywood base plate. Find the small, black rectangular "Motion Sensor" circuit board. It has a white connector with four wires (red, yellow, white, black). Locate the large, 5mm thick, round plywood base plate. It has a central hole, four large rounded cutouts, and two small screw holes near the center. Place the motion sensor flat on top of the plywood base plate, aligning the two holes on the sensor with the two small screw holes on the plate. Ensure the wires point towards one of the large rounded cutouts. Use the two #4-40 brass screws to fasten the sensor to the plate. Other parts shown are four curved plywood brackets, four small three-lobed plywood connectors, and two black rubber O-rings, which will be assembled in subsequent steps to form a structure around the base plate.

g.title: Inventory of Kit Components
g.desc: This instruction area is an inventory list of all the parts you'll need for this kit. Before you begin assembly, carefully locate and gather the following items. You will need: one flat, 5mm laser-cut plywood 'Center Base' (likely the largest single plywood piece); four identical, flat, 5mm laser-cut plywood 'Sides' (these will feel like sturdy rectangular or similarly shaped panels); two identical, flat, 5mm laser-cut plywood 'Caps' (smaller plywood pieces, possibly square or circular); two flexible, circular 'O-Rings' made of rubber or silicone; one rigid 'Sensor' (a small electronic component, possibly plastic-cased with pins); one flexible '4-Pin Cable' (a wire with four strands, often with a connector); and two small, threaded '1/4 inch Screws' (brass metal fasteners). There are no assembly steps shown here, only the list of items to prepare.

g.title: Remove Top and Bottom O-Rings
g.desc: This is the first step of taking apart your kit. First, find the main assembly, which is a round, open cage made of smooth, 5mm thick plywood. You can feel a small, flat circuit board inside with thin wires. Now, feel for two soft, flexible, circular rubber bands, called O-rings. One O-ring is around the top edge of the plywood cage, and the other is around the bottom edge. Gently pull each O-ring outwards and away from the plywood assembly to remove it. Once both O-rings are off, place them in their special spots in your clean-up box.

text.tspan: spot in the clean up box

g.title: Disassemble Outer Wooden Frame
g.desc: This step shows how to take apart the outer wooden frame. You'll feel a central flat wooden piece, likely round or disc-shaped, with a small, rectangular electronic circuit board screwed to its top using two tiny brass screws. A bundle of flexible wires (red, black, white, yellow) connects to the circuit board. Around the central wooden piece, feel for several curved, 5mm thick wooden 'arm' pieces that are slotted into it, forming an outer cage. To disassemble, gently slide or pull these curved wooden 'arm' pieces away from the central piece, freeing them from their slots. The electronic board and the central wood piece should remain together for this step.

g.title: Remove Screws, Disconnect 4-Pin Cable
g.desc: This step illustrates how to remove the motion sensor and its cable. First, find the large, round, flat plywood ring. On its surface, feel for the smaller, flat, rectangular motion sensor board, which is typically black. It will be secured to the plywood ring by two small brass #4-40 screws. Use your screwdriver to unscrew and completely remove both of these. Next, locate the thin 4-pin cable, usually with red, black, yellow, and white wires, connected to a small white plastic connector on one side of the motion sensor board. Gently grasp the cable's white connector and pull it straight away from the motion sensor board to disconnect it.

g.title: Disassembly Complete! Organize Your Pieces.
g.desc: This instruction page signals the end of all disassembly for the kit. The top of the page prominently displays the message 'DISASSEMBLY COMPLETE!'. Below this, there's a reminder instructing you to place each piece you have already disassembled onto its designated spot in the clean-up area, if you haven't done so yet. This step does not show any new pieces or instructions for disassembling components. Instead, it is a final reminder to organize all the individual parts you have taken apart, such as the 5mm thick laser-cut plywood pieces, any plastic or electronic components, and the #4-40 brass hardware (screws and nuts) back into their proper storage locations. You should feel for the distinct textures and shapes of these different material types as you sort them.

g.title: Organize Disassembled Kit Parts for Storage
g.desc: This blueprint section guides you to organize your kit's components after disassembly. You should have one large, circular plywood 'Center Base' with a cross-shaped opening in its middle and four small tabs on its outer edge. Next, you'll find two smaller plywood 'Caps', each shaped like a rounded cross. There are also four curved plywood 'Sides', each with two small tabs on the inner curve and a notch on the outer curve. Look for two flexible rubber 'O-Rings'. You should also have two small, brass #4-40 'Screws', each 1/4 inch long with a Philips head. Finally, find one flat '4-Pin Cable' with a rectangular connector on one end, and one small, rectangular plastic 'Sensor' with two small circular features on one side.

g.title: Identify Center Base Component
g.desc: This is one large, flat, circular piece of 5mm thick plywood. Feel its smooth outer edge; you will find eight small, evenly spaced rectangular slots cut inwards. In the center of the piece, there are four large, smooth, rounded-corner holes arranged like a cross, forming a solid plus shape in the middle. The holes are symmetrical.

g.title: Two Four-Lobed Cap Pieces
g.desc: Find two identical pieces of 5mm thick laser-cut plywood. Each piece is roughly circular, but its edge is not smooth. As you run your finger around the edge of one piece, you will feel four large, U-shaped cut-outs or indentations that are evenly spaced around the circular perimeter. These cut-outs make the piece feel like a rounded cross or a gear with four wide, rounded teeth. These two pieces are your 'caps'.

g.title: Curved Side Pieces with Tabs
g.desc: This is one of four identical 5mm thick laser-cut plywood pieces. It has a wide, curved arch shape, like an upside-down 'U'. Feel along the outer, longer curve for three small rectangular tabs that stick out. One tab is at the very top center of the arch. The other two tabs are located on the lower sides of the curve, one on the left and one on the right. At each bottom end of the arch, you'll feel a distinctive shape with two U-shaped 'feet'. To orient the piece, hold it so the single top tab points upwards, and the two U-shaped feet at each end point downwards.

g.title: Locate Two Rubber O-Rings
g.desc: Locate two O-rings. These are flexible rubber rings, distinct from the rigid plywood or plastic parts. Each O-ring is a smooth, circular piece, roughly 2 inches in diameter, and feels like a thick rubber band. They are symmetrical and have no specific orientation.

g.title: Locate Two 1/4-inch Brass Screws
g.desc: These are two small brass screws, also known as #4-40 screws. You can identify them by feeling their threaded shaft, which is a small rod with tiny ridges or bumps spiraling down its length. At one end, there's a flat, round head with a slot or a cross-shaped indentation. This is where a screwdriver fits. At the other end, the shaft is blunt or slightly pointed. These particular screws are 1/4 inch long, making them quite short. To confirm, carefully feel for two screws that are short and have these features, distinguishing them from longer screws or bolts.

g.title: Identify One 4-Pin Cable
g.desc: This is one flexible electronic cable. Run your fingers along its length to feel four individual wires bundled together. On one end, you will find a distinct, rectangular plastic 'Grove' connector. This connector has a small flat edge or ridge on one side, which helps you orient it correctly. The other end of the cable (not fully shown in this image) has four separate, smaller plastic housings, each containing a single metal pin.

g.title: Find the Motion Sensor Module
g.desc: This is one sensor module. Feel for a small, flat rectangle, probably made of plastic. On one of the shorter ends, there will be a distinct, slightly thicker, rectangular plastic plug. On each of the longer sides, near the end with the plug, you will find a tiny round hole.
